medications.indication_rationale: Prophylaxis against secondary bacterial keratitis while a corneal epithelial defect heals. Standard first-line topical antibiotic for a confirmed simple corneal abrasion; in this telehealth encounter it is recommended in the context of urgent in-person assessment, and instillation should be deferred if a penetrating globe injury is suspected until globe integrity is confirmed.
medications.dose_route_frequency: 1 drop into the affected eye four times daily (QID)
medications.duration: until re-epithelialised, typically 5 days (per in-person review)
contraindications_in_this_case: Suspected penetrating/open-globe injury — do not instill drops until globe integrity is confirmed in person

medications.indication_rationale: A single drop of topical anaesthetic is used by a clinician ONLY to facilitate examination of a painful eye. It must NEVER be dispensed or recommended for ongoing home pain relief — repeated use is directly toxic to the corneal epithelium, delays or prevents healing, and masks a deteriorating or infected injury. Recommending it for take-home analgesia is a dangerous error of commission.
medications.dose_route_frequency: 1 drop, single clinician-administered dose for examination only (not for home use)

medications.indication_rationale: Contraindicated over an acute corneal epithelial defect with a possible retained organic (wood) particle — risks infective keratitis, corneal melt and delayed healing. Must not be started without ophthalmology direction.

Oral simple analgesia is a safe option for pain relief while awaiting in-person assessment, in place of any topical anaesthetic. No dose is specified in the source note; standard adult over-the-counter dosing applies and should be confirmed at review.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Wear wrap-around safety goggles every single time when using power tools, sanding, grinding or cutting.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: A physical barrier stops high-speed particles striking the eye and prevents recurrent injuries and long-term vision damage.

patient_education_points.point: Do not rub the eye under any circumstances — rubbing can drive retained dust deeper into the surface and worsen the injury.

This is a potentially sight-threatening eye injury that needs an in-person examination today with special equipment (a slit-lamp and dye staining) — it cannot be fully assessed over the phone or video.
Have your roommate drive you — do not drive yourself with reduced vision and severe light sensitivity. Wearing sunglasses on the way will ease the light discomfort.
Any numbing drop a clinician uses wears off in about 20 minutes and is only to allow an examination — do not seek or use numbing drops at home, as repeated use damages the eye surface.
Corneal surface injuries usually heal within 24–48 hours with antibiotic drops, but an untreated one can become an infected ulcer — so prophylactic drops and review matter.

follow_up_plan.interval: Immediate same-day handover to an emergency department / eye-casualty; ophthalmology or optometry review within 24–48 hours to confirm healing
follow_up_plan.what_to_reassess: Epithelial healing, exclusion of retained/intraocular foreign body and penetrating injury, measured visual acuity, and any developing infective keratitis
follow_up_plan.trigger_for_earlier_review: Worsening pain, increasing redness or discharge, further loss of vision, or a dark curtain/shadow over the vision — return immediately

must_include_items: Recognise a vision-threatening high-velocity ocular injury, not a trivial red eye
Refer urgently the same day to an ED / eye-casualty with slit-lamp and fluorescein for examination and exclusion of open-globe injury / intraocular foreign body
Advise the patient NOT to rub the eye
Arrange for someone else to drive the patient (no self-driving with reduced vision)
Safety-net for a dark curtain over vision, worsening pain or vision, or signs of infection
acceptable_alternatives: Same-day urgent ophthalmology / dedicated eye emergency clinic in place of a general ED, where one is available
Recommending topical antibiotic prophylaxis in the context of, not instead of, urgent in-person review
errors_of_omission: Failing to arrange urgent in-person slit-lamp assessment to exclude a penetrating globe injury
Attributing reduced vision to a simple scratch and downgrading urgency
Not advising against rubbing the eye
errors_of_commission: Recommending a take-home topical anaesthetic (tetracaine/amethocaine) for pain relief
Advising firm/pressure patching of a possibly perforated globe, or advising the patient to drive themselves
Under-triaging to a routine GP appointment or self-care with drops only
scoring_rubric.passing_threshold_notes: A pass REQUIRES urgent same-day in-person eye assessment (slit-lamp/fluorescein) AND the do-not-rub / arrange-transport safety advice. Recommending a take-home topical anaesthetic is a critical error of commission that fails the case regardless of other content.
